chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: In this video, we will learn about CSS media queries and responsive web design.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Responsive web design means your website looks good on all devices.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: It should look good on mobile phones, tablets, laptops, and desktop screens.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Media queries are the foundation of responsive web design in CSS.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: A media query applies styles based on the result of one or more media expressions.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: The basic syntax is @media screen and max-width 768px.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: This means the styles inside will only apply when screen width is 768 pixels or less.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Common breakpoints are 576px for phones, 768px for tablets, 992px for laptops, 1200px for desktops.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Mobile-first design means you write styles for mobile first, then add media queries for larger screens.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Desktop-first design means you write styles for desktop first, then override for smaller screens.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Mobile-first is generally recommended as more people browse on mobile devices now.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: The viewport meta tag is essential for responsive design to work on mobile devices.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Always add meta name viewport content width device-width initial-scale 1 in your HTML head.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: You can also use media queries to change layout completely on different screen sizes.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: For example, a sidebar layout on desktop can become a stacked layout on mobile.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: The combination of Flexbox, Grid, and Media Queries gives you complete control over responsive layouts.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Let me show you a practical responsive design example from scratch.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: We will create a responsive card layout that changes from 3 columns to 2 to 1.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Using CSS Grid with auto-fit and minmax, we can even avoid media queries for simple responsive grids.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Responsive design is not optional anymore, it is a requirement for every website.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Google also considers mobile-friendliness as a ranking factor in search results.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Always test your website on different screen sizes before deploying.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: Browser DevTools have a device toolbar that lets you simulate different screen sizes.

chunks.title: CSS Media Queries & Responsive Design
chunks.text: In the next video, we will start learning JavaScript which is the programming language of the web.

text: In this video, we will learn about CSS media queries and responsive web design. Responsive web design means your website looks good on all devices. It should look good on mobile phones, tablets, laptops, and desktop screens. Media queries are the foundation of responsive web design in CSS. A media query applies styles based on the result of one or more media expressions. The basic syntax is @media screen and max-width 768px. This means the styles inside will only apply when screen width is 768 pixels or less. Common breakpoints are 576px for phones, 768px for tablets, 992px for laptops, 1200px for desktops. Mobile-first design means you write styles for mobile first, then add media queries for larger screens. Desktop-first design means you write styles for desktop first, then override for smaller screens. Mobile-first is generally recommended as more people browse on mobile devices now. The viewport meta tag is essential for responsive design to work on mobile devices. Always add meta name viewport content width device-width initial-scale 1 in your HTML head. You can also use media queries to change layout completely on different screen sizes. For example, a sidebar layout on desktop can become a stacked layout on mobile. The combination of Flexbox, Grid, and Media Queries gives you complete control over responsive layouts. Let me show you a practical responsive design example from scratch. We will create a responsive card layout that changes from 3 columns to 2 to 1. Using CSS Grid with auto-fit and minmax, we can even avoid media queries for simple responsive grids. Responsive design is not optional anymore, it is a requirement for every website. Google also considers mobile-friendliness as a ranking factor in search results. Always test your website on different screen sizes before deploying. Browser DevTools have a device toolbar that lets you simulate different screen sizes. In the next video, we will start learning JavaScript which is the programming language of the web.
